one_line_takeaway: A complexity-theory paper that pins down when certified machine unlearning is actually cheaper than retraining, proving the first minimax lower and upper bounds on the 'unlearning complexity ratio' and mapping three regimes (trivial / efficient / inefficient) governed by target risk, privacy budget, and the r_f^2-scaling forget fraction.
key_finding: There is a sharp threshold: for small forget ratios r_f << 1, efficient unlearning is impossible when the target excess risk e falls below ~ r_f^2 (1 + d*kappa_{eps,delta}^2) e0 (below this the ratio is bounded away from zero, Theorem 2 / IR) and possible above it, with the Noise-and-Fine-Tune upper bound giving a ratio of O((r_f/(1-r_f))^2 (1 + d*kappa^2) e0/e) (Theorem 3 / ER). Crucially the cost scales with r_f^2 (the SQUARE of the forgotten fraction), so forgetting a small fraction is cheap. Corollary 1 gives an impossibility result: as e -> 0, unlearning cannot asymptotically outperform retraining regardless of privacy strength, and since exact unlearning corresponds to kappa = infinity, exact unlearning (within this first-order, retain-only framework) yields no asymptotic gain. In a realistic regime where e ~ 1/n and kappa ~ 1, the ratio is ~ r_f^2 * d * n, so one can cheaply delete up to ~sqrt(n/d) points, and if r_f ~ 1/n the ratio is ~ d/n (very efficient in under-parameterized settings).
core_contribution: The paper introduces the 'unlearning complexity ratio' T^U_e / T^S_e (number of stochastic gradient steps to unlearn a forget set, divided by the number to retrain from scratch to the same target excess risk e) and derives the FIRST minimax upper and lower bounds on it for strongly-convex, L-Lipschitz losses under a differential-privacy-style unlearning constraint, in the setting where the forget data is inaccessible during unlearning. From these bounds it establishes a phase diagram with three regimes as a function of target excess risk e, privacy-constraint strength kappa_{eps,delta}, and forget ratio r_f: Trivial (TR, unlearning is free because adding Gaussian noise suffices), Efficient (ER, unlearning provably beats retraining, cost scaling with r_f^2), and Inefficient (IR, gradient-based unlearning cannot asymptotically beat retraining). The lower bound (Theorem 2) is claimed as the first lower bound for unlearning time, answering an open problem posed by Allouah et al. (2024). These are the paper's OWN contributions; prior comparisons to the scratch baseline (Izzo et al. 2021, Chourasia & Shah 2023, Allouah et al. 2024) are cited Related Work that bound only specific algorithms, whereas this paper bounds the best possible algorithm over a function class.
threat_model: The relevant adversary is a membership-inference attacker (MIA) attempting to determine whether specific data points remained influential in a deployed model after a data-deletion (GDPR/CCPA 'right to erasure') request. The paper adopts a differential-privacy-based unlearning guarantee ((eps,delta)-Unlearning, Definition 2, a slight strengthening of Ginart et al. 2019's reference unlearning) whose value is provable robustness against ANY MIA over the forget set. Importantly, this is a complexity-theory paper: it does NOT run attacks, and the 'threat' enters only through the privacy budget (eps,delta) / kappa_{eps,delta} that constrains admissible unlearning algorithms. The security contribution is characterizing the compute cost of achieving that certified privacy, not proposing or breaking an attack.
limitations: Restricted to strongly-convex and Lipschitz losses on a bounded ball (Assumption 1), which the authors acknowledge does not directly apply to neural networks. Only first-order, retain-set-only algorithms are covered, so exact/sharding-based methods and methods that access the forget set are excluded (and left as open problems). There is a gap between bounds: the sqrt(d) dependence in the upper bound (Theorem 3, inherited from DP noise a la Bassily et al. 2014) is NOT matched by the lower bound, and the authors explicitly leave closing this discrepancy to future work. The 'Noise and Fine-Tune' algorithm used for the upper bound is admittedly possibly sub-optimal, so ER-regime ratios could be even smaller. Experiments are small-scale (linear regression / Digits, r_f = 10^-2, 50 runs) and serve only to validate the theory, not to benchmark against competing unlearning systems.

datasets_used: Synthetic 'worst-case' loss for linear regression in R^d (L=25, mu=1), derived from the hard function of the Theorem 2 lower-bound proof
Digits handwritten-digit dataset (subset of Alpaydin and Kaynak 1998), used with cross-entropy + L2-regularized linear model, batch size 64, decaying learning rate
Forget ratio r_f = 10^-2 in all experiments; results averaged over 50 runs; T swept logarithmically from 1 to 10^6; e and kappa_{eps,delta} swept over 10^-2 to 10^2
quantitative_results: Retraining complexity: T^S_e = Theta(e0/e) with e0 = L^2/(8mu); learning is trivial (T^S_e = T^U_e = 0) when e >= e0 (Lemma 4.1, 4.2)
Trivial regime (Theorem 1): ratio = 0 when e in [ r_f/(1-r_f) * (r_f/(1-r_f) + sqrt(d)*kappa) * e0 , e0 ]
Inefficient regime (Theorem 2, delta in [1e-8, eps]): ratio = Omega(1) when e < c*(r_f/(1-r_f))^2*(1+kappa^2)*e0 for a universal constant c
Efficient regime (Theorem 3, Noise-and-Fine-Tune): ratio = O((r_f/(1-r_f))^2 * (1 + d*kappa^2) * e0/e), i.e. scaling with r_f^2
Corollary 1: liminf_{e->0} T^U_e/T^S_e > 0 (no asymptotic gain as risk -> 0; implies exact unlearning, kappa=infinity, gains nothing here)
Practical estimate: with e ~ 1/n and kappa ~ 1, ratio ~ r_f^2 * d * n; deletable points ~ sqrt(n/d); if r_f ~ 1/n then ratio ~ d/n

Empirical phase diagrams (Fig. 2) reproduce all three regimes; ER-region ratios observed well below 1; ER/TR frontier appears as slope-1 line (log-log) for the synthetic worst-case loss and as a square-root frontier for the smooth real-data loss
baselines_compared: Retraining from scratch via SGD (the 'scratch baseline'), used as T^S_e denominator; SGD chosen because it attains the optimal Theta(e0/e) rate
'Noise and Fine-Tune' unlearning (Algorithm 2, adapted from Neel et al. 2021 perturbed gradient descent), used as the T^U_e estimator and to prove the Theorem 3 upper bound

Cited (not re-run) prior algorithm-specific comparisons: Izzo et al. 2021 (Hessian-based linear-model MU), Chourasia & Shah 2023, Allouah et al. 2024 (non-stochastic GD bounds), Huang & Canonne 2023 (retain-set-inaccessible cannot beat full-dataset DP)
